pi_items.source: Multiple sources including FWS, Wikipedia, and IUCN databases confirm Red Wolf scientific name as Canis rufus
IUCN Red List database and FWS species profile confirm Red Wolf has been listed as Critically Endangered since 1996
FWS species profile confirms Red Wolf listed as Endangered under ESA since 1967 (Endangered Species Preservation Act) and carried into ESA 1973
Multiple sources including FWS, BirdLife, Wikipedia, and NatureServe confirm California Condor scientific name as Gymnogyps californianus

IUCN Red List and BirdLife DataZone confirm California Condor is listed as Critically Endangered. Species was extinct in wild by 1987 but successfully reintroduced with current population of 561-569 birds

FWS species profile and Federal Register documents confirm California Condor listed as Endangered under ESA since March 11, 1967 under ESPA, carried into ESA 1973. Designated as nonessential experimental population in portions of Arizona/Utah/Nevada since October 1996
Multiple sources including FWS, Wikipedia, and IUCN databases confirm Black-footed Ferret scientific name as Mustela nigripes
IUCN Red List database confirms Black-footed Ferret is listed as Endangered on the Red List due to very small and restricted populations
FWS species profile confirms Black-footed Ferret listed as Endangered throughout its range under ESA since 1967, with some portions in western states designated as experimental populations
Multiple sources including International Crane Foundation, FWS, BirdLife, and NatureServe confirm Whooping Crane scientific name as Grus americana
IUCN Red List database confirms Whooping Crane is listed as Endangered due to very small population. FWS and BirdLife DataZone also confirm EN status

FWS species profile confirms Whooping Crane listed as Endangered in federal register in 1970, carried into ESA 1973. Current total population slightly exceeds 830 birds including wild, reintroduced, and captive populations
Multiple sources including IUCN Red List, FWS, USGS, and Marine Mammal Commission confirm Florida Manatee (West Indian Manatee subspecies) scientific name as Trichechus manatus latirostris
IUCN Red List database updated in late 2023 to list West Indian manatee (including Florida subspecies) as Vulnerable due to low number of mature individuals and projected population decline

FWS official press release from March/April 2017 confirms Florida Manatee (West Indian Manatee) was downlisted from Endangered to Threatened status. Current ESA status is Threatened. The metadata lists both statuses, but the current status is Threatened only

privileged_info_summary: All five North American endangered species in this task have been verified against authoritative sources including the IUCN Red List, U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service, and specialized conservation organizations. Scientific names are all correct: Red Wolf (Canis rufus), California Condor (Gymnogyps californianus), Black-footed Ferret (Mustela nigripes), Whooping Crane (Grus americana), and Florida Manatee (Trichechus manatus latirostris). IUCN statuses verified: Red Wolf and California Condor are Critically Endangered (CR), Black-footed Ferret, Whooping Crane are Endangered (EN), and Florida Manatee is Vulnerable (VU) as of late 2023. ESA statuses verified: Red Wolf, California Condor, Black-footed Ferret, and Whooping Crane are all listed as Endangered. Florida Manatee was downlisted from Endangered to Threatened status in April 2017, making its current ESA status Threatened, not Endangered. The data represents real biological facts from authoritative conservation databases, not synthetic data.
